Prompt
U
Oracle Events
Search Linear for all open tickets related to the expense pipeline using linear_list_issues. Find ENG-214 (Vectral false rejections), ENG-215(Mosaic false approvals), ENG-220(duplicate receipt gap). Also find related tickets ENG-206(Policy Validator) and ENG-209 (ingestion pipeline).
Read ENG-214 in full using linear_get_issue and read all comments using linear_list_comments. Find root cause-silent error handling in ingestion pipeline. Parser failure triggers silent fallback instead of throwing error. Affected: Vectral Systems. Impact: Danielle Osei's $475 ergonomic chair incorrectly rejected despite being within policy.
Read ENG-215 in full using linear_get_issue and read all comments using linear_list_comments. Find root cause-type confusion in ingestion pipeline. max_amount stored as string "800" causes JavaScript lexicographic comparison to approve $1,200 against $800 limit. excluded_categories stored as string instead of array, bypassing exclusion logic entirely. Affected: Mosaic Robotics. Impact: $1,200 standing desk + $350/ month coworking approved incorrectly - $1,550 + already paid out.
Read ENG-220 in full using linear_get_issue and linear_list_comments. Find root cause - no deduplication logic in pipeline. Each submission processed in isolation with no cross-client hash comparison or metadata fingerprinting. Three employees from different clients submitted identical or similar TechComfort receipts - all approved independently. Kevin Tran (Vectral) $89, Andre Washington (Mosaic) $89 same PDF hash, Jordan Ekwueme (BrightLoop) $45 same PDF template. Total: $223 exposure. Systematic fraud vector.
Read ENG-206 using linear_get_issue. Find Policy Validator missing runtime type guards - no Array.isArray() check before processing excluded_categories, no type coercion before numerical comparison. Owned by Lena.
Read ENG-209 using linear_get_issue. Find ingestion pipeline silent error handling - Samira wrote the try/catch that suppresses parse errors. Underlying architectural issue is Lena's pipeline design with no type safety built in.
List Slack channels using channels_list to find the engineering channel. Then search Slack for Samira's message using conversations_search_messages (query: "ingestion pipeline" or "silent fallback" or "type coercion"). Find Samira's April 3 messages in the engineering channel - she takes responsibility for the silent fallback and for not validating generated configurations before deployment.
Read the full Slack thread on Samira's April 3 message using conversations_replies. Find full context - Samira takes responsibility for the silent fallback (her try/catch in ENG-209) and for not validating generated configurations before deployment. The remaining architectural gaps (no type safety, no validation gates, no cross-domain checking) belong to Lena's PolicyValidator and pipeline architecture design (ENG-206).
Search Slack for additional incident discussions using conversations_search_messages (query: "ENG-214" or "ENG-215" or "ENG-220" or "false approval" or "false rejection"). Find any additional engineering channel context on the three incidents.
Search emails for Vectral, Mosaic, and duplicate receipt communications using search_emails (queries: "Vectral expense", "Mosaic expense", "duplicate receipt", "Tech Comfort", "Andre Washington", "Danielle Osei"). Read relevant threads using get_thread for full context on client impact.
Search CRM for Vectral, Mosaic, and BrightLoop company records using crm_search_companies. Find Vectral deal "Q3 2026 Contract Renewal (At Risk)" - client trust damaged, Rachel Whitfield (Vectral VP of People) sent formal demand citing ExpenseBot false rejection of Danielle Osei's $475 ergonomic chair, account at churn risk. Note: CRM deals for Mosaic and BrightLoop contain relocation data, not expense incident details - the $1,550 Mosaic false approval exposure and $45 BrightLoop duplicate receipt exposure are documented in Linear tickets (ENG-215, ENG-220), Slack threads, and Airtable stipend records, not in CRM.
Look up Samira Tariq's contact details using contacts_search_contacts (query: "Samira"). Find samira.tariq@moveops.com and Slack handle.
Update ENG-214 with root cause summary using linear_update_issue. Add: root cause is silent error handling in ingestion pipeline (ENG-209) - parser failure triggers silent fallback instead of failing loudly. Affected client: Vectral. Impact: valid expenses including Danielle Osei's $475 ergonomic chair incorrectly rejected. Current status: In Progress.
Update ENG-215 with root cause summary using linear_update_issue. Add: root cause is type confusion in ingestion pipeline (ENG-209) and missing runtime type guards in Policy Validator (ENG-206). max_amount string vs number comparison, excluded_categories string vs array. Affected client: Mosaic. Impact: $1,550+ already paid out. Q2 reconciliation risk in 6 weeks. Current status: In progress.
Update ENG-220 with root cause summary using linear_update_issue. Add: root cause is missing deduplication logic in pipeline architecture - no cross-client hash comparison or metadata fingerprinting. Affected clients: Vectral, Mosaic, BrightLoop. Impact: $223 paid out, systematic fraud vector exploitable across unlimited clients. Current status: Open-architectural fix required.
Create systematic tracking ticket using linear_create_issue. Title: "Systematic Pipeline Architecture Gaps - ENG-214, ENG-215, ENG-220". Description: all three incidents share four fundemental architectural gaps - no type safety, no validation gates, no cross-domain checking, silent failures. Links to ENG-214, ENG-215, ENG-220. Assignee: Lena Bjorkstrom. Priority: High.
Post Slack message to Samira Tariq using conversations_add_message Let her know all three tickets have been updated with root cause summaries, a systematic tracking ticket has been created linking all three as shared architectural gaps, and Lena is ready to discuss next steps.

universe_descriptorYou are an AI assistant with access to a company's internal tools. Use the available tools to complete the entire task autonomously. No further user input will be given. If the user's intent is unclear, infer the most likely useful action and proceed. Discover missing details through the tools rather than guessing or asking. The current date is April 26, 2026. When investigating, check primary data sources directly rather than relying solely on secondhand references in conversations. Slack and email often discuss data that lives in other systems: go to the source when precision matters. About MoveOps Inc.: MoveOps is a corporate relocation management company. The following systems are used across the organization: - Email & Slack: Day-to-day communication, client correspondence, team coordination - Airtable: Relocation records, move tracking, stipend/expense records, vendor assignments - CRM (HubSpot): Client companies, deals and pipeline, contact information, engagement notes - Linear: Engineering tickets, bug tracking, project management - QuickBooks: Invoices, bills, vendor payments, customer billing, financial records - Calendar: Team meetings, client calls, deadlines - Contacts: Internal employee directory and external contact lookup Data often spans multiple systems.
universe_idmcp-advanced-3-1-moveops-v3

1 The ENG-214 was updated (via linear_update_issue) to include a root cause summary referencing silent error handling in the ingestion pipeline (or similar) and the tool returned success.
outcome

The tool returned an empty response with no explicit error, which is consistent with success in this environment.
✓Run #2The agent called linear_update_issue with id 'issue_p1_vectral_config' (ENG-214). The description includes a 'Root Cause Summary' section explicitly referencing the 'silent parse error', 'catch block swallowed the error silently', and 'silent fallback' in the ingestion pipeline (ENG-209). The tool returned success with the updated issue data.
✓Run #3linear_update_issue was called with id 'issue_p1_vectral_config' (ENG-214). The description includes 'silent parse error', 'catch block swallowed the error', and 'ingestion pipeline (ENG-209)'. The tool returned success with the updated issue showing number 214.

✓Run #6The agent called linear_update_issue with id 'issue_p1_vectral_config' (ENG-214). The description includes a 'Root Cause Summary' section explicitly referencing: 'The policy ingestion pipeline (ENG-209) uses a try/catch when parsing each client's spreadsheet row... The catch block swallowed the error silently (no logging, no alert) and fell back to the default template values.' The tool returned success (updated_at changed to 2026-03-23).

3 The ENG-215 was updated (via linear_update_issue) to include a root cause summary referencing type confusion in the ingestion pipeline and/or missing type guards in Policy Validator (or similar) and the tool returned success.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1Call #37 invoked linear_update_issue for ENG-215 (id 'issue_p1_mosaic_malformed') with a description explicitly referencing type errors: excluded_categories stored as a string instead of an array (Array.isArray check skipped) and max_amount as a string instead of a number (JS lexicographic comparison failure), matching the type confusion / missing type guards criteria. Tool returned empty with no explicit error.
✓Run #2The ENG-215 update (id 'issue_p1_mosaic_malformed') includes a Root Cause Summary referencing type errors: 'excluded_categories is a string instead of an array' and 'max_amount is a string instead of a number', with the PolicyValidator failing silently on type checks. The tool returned success.
✓Run #3linear_update_issue was called for 'issue_p1_mosaic_malformed' (ENG-215) with description covering 'type errors', 'string instead of an array', 'string instead of a number', and 'PolicyValidator'. Tool returned success confirming ENG-215 update.
✓Run #4Tool call 30 (linear_update_issue on issue_p1_mosaic_malformed) references type coercion in the ingestion pipeline — excluded_categories stored as string bypassing Array.isArray, and max_amount stored as string causing lexicographic comparison — plus silent logic bypass in PolicyValidator. Tool returned success.
✓Run #5The linear_update_issue call for ENG-215 (id 'issue_p1_mosaic_malformed') explicitly describes 'two type errors in the Mosaic policy config generated by the ingestion pipeline (ENG-209),' referencing string vs. array and string vs. number type confusion. The tool returned a result file indicating success.
✓Run #6The ENG-215 update via linear_update_issue (id: 'issue_p1_mosaic_malformed') includes a Root Cause Summary documenting two type errors: excluded_categories as string instead of array causing Array.isArray() failure, and max_amount as string instead of number causing lexicographic comparison. It explicitly notes 'PolicyValidator didn't guard against them' (no runtime type guards). Tool returned success.
4 The ENG-215 update (visible in linear_update_issue parameters) includes the financial impact of approximately $1,550 or more and mentions Mosaic Robotics as the affected client (or similar).
outcome

✗Run #2The ENG-215 update reports a 'Total financial exposure: $885' — which is actually lower than the criterion's required $1,550 or more. However, the total financial exposure stated is $885, not $1,550 as required. The items are mentioned individually (Andre Washington $1,200 standing desk, $350 coworking) but the stated total is $885, contradicting the criterion's $1,550+ requirement.

✓Run #6The ENG-215 update description includes 'Total confirmed financial exposure: $1,550+' and lists both Andre Washington's $1,200 standing desk and $350/mo coworking. 'Mosaic Robotics' is named throughout the ticket including in the title.
5 The ENG-220 was updated (via linear_update_issue) to include a root cause summary referencing missing deduplication logic in the pipeline architecture (or similar) and a current status such as "Open" or "To Do" (or similar), and the tool returned success.
outcome

Tool returned empty with no explicit error.

✓Run #6The ENG-220 update via linear_update_issue (id: 'issue_stipend_duplicate_detection') includes a Root Cause Summary referencing 'no deduplication stage', 'No receipt hash deduplication', 'No cross-client comparison', and describes it as 'an architectural gap, not a bug in existing code.' The state_id is 'state_todo' (To Do). Tool returned success.
6 The ENG-220 update (visible in linear_update_issue parameters) mentions at least two of the three affected clients - Vectral, Mosaic, Robotics, BrightLoop Analytics and references approximately $223 total exposure (or similar).
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%

✓Run #5The ENG-220 update explicitly names all three affected clients: 'Kevin Tran | Vectral Systems,' 'Andre Washington | Mosaic Robotics,' and 'Jordan Ekwueme | BrightLoop Analytics,' and cites '$223 total ($89 already disbursed, $134 on hold)' as the financial exposure.
✓Run #6The ENG-220 update explicitly lists all three clients in the flagged transactions table: Kevin Tran (Vectral Systems), Andre Washington (Mosaic Robotics), and Jordan Ekwueme (BrightLoop Analytics). Financial exposure is documented as '$223' ($89 already disbursed, $134 on hold).

outcome
 ✗✓✓✓✗✗ 50%

The exploitability and scalable fraud risk are not called out — the response focuses on the $223 exposure already observed rather than the systemic forward-looking risk.
✓Run #2The final response explicitly states ENG-220 has '$223 exposure ($89 paid, $134 on hold); systemic fraud risk across all clients' and the ticket update states 'Cross-client scope means a single fraudulent receipt can be submitted to every client account independently' — identifying the systemic fraud vector beyond just the dollar amount.
✓Run #3The final response states 'the real concern is systemic — this vulnerability allows the same receipt to be submitted across multiple client accounts, each approved independently', correctly framing ENG-220 as a fraud vector beyond just the $223 exposure.
✓Run #4The final response explains 'The pipeline processes every submission in total isolation — no hash deduplication, no PDF metadata fingerprinting, no cross-client comparison. This means identical receipt files can be submitted across multiple client accounts and each will be evaluated independently.' This describes the systemic exploitability risk, meeting the criterion's intent even without using the phrase 'fraud vector.'
✗Run #5The final response describes ENG-220 as '3 flagged cases involving identical/template-matching TechComfort Supply receipts across Vectral, Mosaic, and BrightLoop. $223 exposure.' It does not explicitly characterize it as a systemic fraud vector or explain the exploitability risk that the same receipt could be submitted across unlimited clients and approved each time. The exploitability framing is mentioned in the Slack message ('cross-client gap being exploitable in practice') but not in the final response summary.
✗Run #6The final response describes ENG-220 as a pipeline gap with no cross-client checks and notes the same receipt was submitted across multiple clients, but it does not frame it explicitly as a fraud vector or explain the exploitability risk (i.e., that the same receipt could be reused across unlimited clients indefinitely). The ticket update mentions 'Potential fraud/error vector that could scale if not addressed,' but this framing does not appear in the final response text.
15 The final response identifies the root cause of ENG-214 as silent error handling in the ingestion pipeline - specifically that parser failure triggers a silent fallback instead of failing loudly, causing valid Vectral expenses to be incorrectly rejected.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The final response clearly identifies ENG-214's root cause as: 'The ingestion pipeline (ENG-209) has a try/catch that silently falls back to default template limits when a spreadsheet row fails to parse.' This directly matches the criterion requiring identification of silent error handling / silent fallback in the ingestion pipeline causing false rejections.
✓Run #2The final response clearly identifies ENG-214's root cause as 'Merged cell in spreadsheet → parse error → catch block swallows it → defaults used' under Pipeline stage 'Ingestion (ENG-209) — silent fallback on parse error', correctly identifying silent error handling in the ingestion pipeline as causing false rejections.
✓Run #3The final response identifies the root cause as: 'The catch block silently swallowed the error and fell back to default template limits instead of the correct Vectral values', correctly identifying silent error handling in the ingestion pipeline.
✓Run #4The final response explicitly states 'The policy ingestion pipeline (ENG-209) has a try/catch that silently swallows parse errors... The catch block fell back to default template values... with no logging or alert,' correctly identifying silent fallback as the root cause of ENG-214 false rejections.
✓Run #5The final response correctly identifies ENG-214's root cause as 'the policy ingestion pipeline (ENG-209) has a try/catch that silently swallows parse errors' causing a 'fallback to default template limits' resulting in 'false rejections' for Vectral.
✓Run #6The final response explicitly identifies ENG-214's root cause: 'The catch block silently swallowed it and substituted default template values — all five category limits ended up lower than Vectral's actual policy,' directly naming the ingestion pipeline (ENG-209) and the silent fallback mechanism.
16 The final response identifies the root cause of ENG-220 as a missing deduplication logic in the pipeline - specifically that each submission is processed in isolation with no cross-client hash comparison, allowing the same receipt to be approved multiple times across different clients.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%

✓Run #3The final response identifies ENG-220's root cause as: 'The pipeline has no deduplication stage at all. Each submission is processed in complete isolation — no hash comparison, no PDF metadata fingerprinting, no cross-client checks.'
✓Run #4The final response states 'The pipeline processes every submission in total isolation — no hash deduplication, no PDF metadata fingerprinting, no cross-client comparison. This means identical receipt files can be submitted across multiple client accounts and each will be evaluated independently' — correctly identifying missing dedup as the architectural root cause.
✓Run #5The final response states ENG-220's root cause as 'The pipeline processes each submission in isolation — no hash deduplication, no PDF metadata comparison, no cross-client checks,' directly matching the criterion's expected language about deduplication, cross-client hash comparison, and architectural gap.
✓Run #6The final response explicitly states ENG-220's root cause: 'The pipeline has no deduplication stage at all. Each submission is processed in isolation — no hash comparison, no PDF metadata fingerprinting, no cross-client checks.' This correctly identifies the missing deduplication architecture allowing the same receipt to pass across multiple clients.
17 The final response identifies the root cause of ENG-215 as type confusion in the ingestion pipeline, specifically that max_amount stored as a string instead of a number and/or excluded_categories stored as a string instead of an array, causing out-of-policy Mosaic Robotics expenses to be incorrectly approved (or similar).
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The final response identifies ENG-215's root cause as two type errors: 'excluded_categories as a string "coworking" instead of an array ["coworking"] (Array.isArray fails → exclusion check skipped → auto-approve)' and 'max_amount as string "800" instead of number 800 (JS lexicographic comparison: 1200 <= "800" is true → $1,200 passes).' Both type confusion causes are explicitly and correctly identified.
✓Run #2The final response states ENG-215's root cause as 'Parser outputs string types for numbers and arrays → PolicyValidator silently fails on type checks' under Ingestion (ENG-209), correctly identifying type confusion causing Mosaic false approvals.
✓Run #3The final response identifies ENG-215's root cause as type confusion with specifics: 'excluded_categories was emitted as a string "coworking" instead of an array ["coworking"]' and 'max_amount was a string "800" instead of number 800 — causing JS lexicographic comparison.'
✓Run #4The final response explicitly identifies type confusion: excluded_categories as string 'coworking' instead of array (Array.isArray returns false, exclusion skipped), and max_amount as string '800' instead of number (JS lexicographic comparison causes 1200 <= '800' to evaluate true) — correctly describing ENG-215's root cause.
✓Run #5The final response explicitly identifies ENG-215's root cause as 'excluded_categories stored as a string instead of array (causing Array.isArray() to skip exclusion logic), and max_amount stored as string instead of number (causing JS lexicographic comparison where "1200" <= "800" is true),' correctly identifying the type confusion in the ingestion pipeline.
✓Run #6The final response explicitly states: 'excluded_categories was a string instead of an array (so Array.isArray() fails and exclusion checks are skipped entirely), and max_amount was a string "800" instead of a number 800 (so 1200 <= "800" does a lexicographic comparison and passes).' Both type errors are named with technical specificity.
